segments.title: Operation Y-Go and the limits of delaying victory
segments.summary: Operation Y-Go is used to show that even a successful surprise attack on American carriers could only delay, not reverse, the Pacific war.
segments.body: The discussion compares a hoped-for carrier raid with Pearl Harbor while stressing the changed industrial balance of 1944. American carrier losses could be replaced in months, whereas Japan could not restore equivalent strength. The contrast frames Tan No. 2 as an attempt to gain time without a credible path to changing the war's outcome.

evidence.note: The lecture explains Y-Go, replacement capacity, and why a carrier strike could only delay the war.

segments.title: Kantai Kessen and the danger of planning for a foolish enemy
segments.summary: The failure of Kantai Kessen becomes a warning against strategies that work only if an opponent keeps making mistakes.
segments.body: The lecture treats the Philippine Sea as the last opportunity for the decisive-battle concept and a demonstration of its weakness. A viable plan must assume a capable, committed opponent rather than one who keeps advancing into an arranged trap. That assumption matters because Tan No. 2 demanded American complacency while also demanding perfect Japanese execution.

evidence.note: The lecture links Kantai Kessen to assumptions about enemy behaviour and strategic preparation.

segments.title: Operation Tan's small-strike problem
segments.summary: An earlier plan for just 54 aircraft reveals how little margin Japan had for a surprise raid on a major anchorage.
segments.body: The original Operation Tan concept called for reconnaissance from Truk followed by a modest mixed air strike. Its prospects depended on unusual surprise and still offered no certainty of major results. The discussion also explains why shallow-water torpedo attack required more than simply altering the torpedo, using British practice as the comparison.

evidence.note: The lecture describes the 54-aircraft concept and the technical problem of torpedo attacks in harbour.

segments.title: C6N reconnaissance undone by serviceability
segments.summary: The C6N Saiun promised long-range reconnaissance, but the move to Truk left only one aircraft reliably available.
segments.body: The C6N was designed to solve the limitations of cruiser floatplane reconnaissance, but it required careful maintenance and specialized support. Of seven aircraft sent toward Truk, losses, defects, and a landing accident quickly reduced availability. The resulting reconnaissance plan depended on one serviceable aircraft and a submarine-delivered supply effort.

evidence.note: The lecture describes the C6N's role, the delivery losses, and the lack of maintenance support at Truk.

segments.title: Ulithi and the wrong scale for a penny-packet raid
segments.summary: Ulithi's immense anchorage exposes the mismatch between the American fleet base and Japan's small proposed raid.
segments.body: Ulithi had become a major forward base with floating docks, supply ships, escorts, battleships, and multiple kinds of carriers. The lecture argues that counting only the carriers hid the depth of the force being targeted. A small force sent piecemeal against such a concentration would be expended without producing strategically meaningful damage.

evidence.note: The lecture describes Ulithi's facilities and fleet concentration, then criticizes penny-packet attacks.

segments.title: Tan No. 2's strike plan had no fuel margin
segments.summary: The planned 24 Ginga kamikaze strike depended on a long weather-avoidance route that made fuel itself part of the weapon's shortfall.
segments.body: The strike force included P1Y Ginga bombers, H8K flying boats for weather and guidance, and submarines assigned to support roles. The lecture stresses that a kamikaze aircraft's remaining fuel contributed to its destructive effect, yet the route left little fuel after diversions. Against a fleet at Ulithi, 24 aircraft were neither a viable mass attack nor a force worth sacrificing in isolation.

evidence.note: The lecture outlines the P1Y and H8K force, the route, fuel burden, and inadequate strike size.

segments.title: Submarine support and rigid planning
segments.summary: The submarine plan added logistics, navigation, and rescue tasks while mechanical failures and rigid orders eroded its usefulness.
segments.body: Three submarines were assigned to a mixture of rescue, supply, and navigation support, making the air operation dependent on another fragile chain. I-366 had to return for repairs, while I-58 was recalled from a Kaiten mission despite being close to its intended release point. The episode illustrates how adherence to a written plan displaced practical judgement about what could still be achieved.

evidence.note: The lecture details the submarine assignments, I-366's defects, and I-58's recall.

segments.title: Reconnaissance of Ulithi and a fragile launch
segments.summary: A C6N reconnaissance flight identified carriers at Ulithi, but bad weather and engine failures began dismantling the strike force on departure.
segments.body: The 5 March reconnaissance found a large carrier concentration and confirmed the scale of the intended target. On D-Day, the H8K pathfinders were delayed by weather and the Ginga launch was already behind schedule. Within hours, five aircraft had turned back and three had crashed, reducing the force before it approached the target.

evidence.note: The lecture covers the C6N sighting, the D-Day launch, weather delays, and early attrition.

segments.title: Navigation error, attrition, and the hit on USS Randolph
segments.summary: Engine trouble and a navigation error left only two aircraft at Ulithi, where one struck USS Randolph with little fuel remaining.
segments.body: The strike shrank from 24 aircraft to eight before its navigational error near Yap, and only two eventually reached Ulithi. One P1Y hit the Essex-class carrier USS Randolph, but its fuel was so depleted that the resulting damage was limited. The other aircraft crashed harmlessly, leaving the operation with heavy Japanese losses and no durable operational result.

evidence.note: The lecture recounts the losses, the navigation error, the Randolph strike, and the raid's limited result.

segments.title: Carrier resilience and the forlorn-hope test
segments.summary: The aftermath contrasts carrier resilience with a true forlorn hope: extreme risk is defensible only when it can unlock a concrete gain.
segments.body: Comparison with later kamikaze damage shows why fuel, multiple impacts, and carrier protection affected the consequences of an air attack. Japanese reports initially imagined a second Pearl Harbor, but follow-up reconnaissance found the carriers still present. The final argument is moral as well as strategic: a high-risk force may have a purpose when it opens a decisive opportunity, but Tan No. 2 lacked both adequate resources and a credible payoff.

evidence.note: The lecture discusses carrier resilience, the false expectation of success, and the comparison with a forlorn hope.
